graph.confusable_reason: Both cause rapid water-soaked foliar blight and plant decline on cucurbits, so a wilting, blighted plant can be read either way.
graph.shared_presentation: Water-soaked, blighted foliage with rapid decline of the plant.

forks.question: Are the leaf lesions angular and bounded by the veins, or large irregular water-soaked blotches?
forks.a_signal: Angular chlorotic-to-brown lesions sharply bounded by leaf veins.
forks.b_signal: Large irregular water-soaked blotches that ignore vein boundaries.

forks.question: Is damage confined to foliage, or do crown, stem, and fruit rot as well?
forks.a_signal: Damage confined to the leaves; crown and fruit stay firm.
forks.b_signal: Crown, stem, and fruit develop soft watery rot and the plant collapses.

forks.question: Examine the lesion undersides in morning humidity versus the crown and fruit.
forks.a_signal: Downy gray-to-purple sporulation on the undersides of vein-bounded lesions.
forks.b_signal: White cottony growth on fruit/crown with soft, watery rot.

forks.question: What conditions preceded it?
forks.a_signal: Cool, humid weather with prolonged dew/leaf wetness.
forks.b_signal: Heavy rain, saturated or poorly drained soil, standing water in low spots.

graph.narrative_a: Because lesions are angular and vein-bounded with downy gray sporulation on the leaf undersides and no crown or fruit rot, I classify this as cucurbit downy mildew rather than Phytophthora blight.
graph.narrative_b: Because there is soft crown and fruit rot with white cottony growth and collapse from saturated soil rather than vein-bounded foliar lesions, I classify this as Phytophthora blight rather than downy mildew.
graph.management_a: Cucurbit downy mildew: apply targeted oomycete fungicides on a tight schedule once regional forecast alerts fire, and use tolerant cultivars.
graph.management_b: Phytophthora blight: improve drainage, avoid overwatering, rotate away from cucurbits and solanaceous crops, and use soil-targeted oomycete fungicides.

lay.farmer_lay_report: My squash leaves suddenly look water-soaked and wilted, like they got scalded, and the plants are going downhill fast in just a few days.
lay.decisive_lay_a: Flip a leaf over: under the angular, blocky spots (the ones boxed in by the leaf veins) you'll see a fine fuzzy gray-to-purplish coating on the underside.
lay.decisive_lay_b: Look at the fruit and the base of the plant near the soil: a white cottony, fluffy growth on a soft, mushy, watery rotten spot.
